segments.s382-g65: گه is an adverb here meaning “sometimes” and scopes over the following noun phrase. بزم زر and رزم تیغ are parallel noun–noun constructions with the ezāfe omitted (a common poetic compression): بزم زر = “gold for the feast” (or “feast of gold”), and رزم تیغ = “sword for battle” (or “battle‑sword”). و simply links the two parallel phrases.
segments.s382-g66: ز is the poetic form of از meaning “from/regarding,” introducing خواهنده “the one who asks / petitioner.” هرگز is the negative adverb “never,” placed before the verb. دارد in ندارد is present 3rd‑singular with the negative prefix ن; دریغ is a noun “reluctance/hesitation” used as the complement: ندارد دریغ = “has no reluctance,” idiomatically “does not withhold / does not begrudge.”
segments.s382-g49: چو here means 'when' (temporal). سخن را بگذاشتم is a first-person past construction: بگذاشتم (I put aside/left off) takes سخن را (the speech) as its object; the object marker را follows سخن. سال بیست follows as a temporal specification 'the twentieth year'—word order is flexible and poetic, so the time phrase comes after the verb phrase.
segments.s382-g50: بدان is split here into ب (prefix) + دان 'know/consider', functioning as an infinitival or purposive 'to know/so as to find out'. تا introduces the purpose/result clause 'in order that / so that'. سزاوار این رنج کیست is a nominal question clause 'who is worthy of this hardship' with کیست (کی + ست) as 'who is' (کی = who; ست = copula 'is').
segments.s382-g51: This is a nominative apposition: the proper name ابوالقاسم is followed by an epithet آن شهریار جهان 'that king (sovereign) of the world' describing him. آن is the demonstrative/adjectival 'that/the' modifying شهریار; جهان is a genitival epithet 'of the world'.
segments.s382-g52: کزو = 'from whom / from which' introduces the source. تازه شد is a passive/simple perfective sense 'became new / was renewed' (تازه + شد 'became'); تاج شاهنشاهان 'the crown of the emperors' is the thing that was made/new. Poetic inversion places تازه شد before its object.
segments.s382-g53: جهاندار محمود is a titled name phrase 'the world-ruler Mahmud'. با introduces accompaniment or characteristic 'with', here listing qualities فر و جود 'glory and generosity' as attributes that accompany him. The coordination و links فر and جود.
segments.s382-g54: که introduces a relative/explanatory clause 'such that/that'. او را is the indirect object 'to him' (object marker را placed after the pronoun). کند is the present/past poetic verb 'makes/causes' and is placed before its subjects (poetic inversion). ماه و کیوان are the logical subjects 'the moon and Kiwan (Saturn)', and سجود is the verbal noun 'prostration' that they perform (i.e., 'they prostrate' or 'they make prostration').
segments.s382-g55: سر نامه را is a locative/positional phrase 'at the head (opening) of the book' (سر = head/top, نامه = book/treatise) with را used for emphasis/marking. نام او تاج گشت is a copular result: 'his name became a crown' (نام او = his name/fame; تاج گشت = 'became a crown'). Word order places the locative phrase before the predication for poetic effect.
segments.s382-g56: به introduces the target/location 'on/unto' (به فرش = on the carpet). دل تیره is a genitival descriptive 'the heart's dark (carpet)' (دل = heart, تیره = dark). چون introduces a simile 'like/as', عاج is 'ivory', and گشت is the copular/transformative verb 'became/turned'. The clause describes a transformation likening the (surface of the) heart to ivory.
segments.s382-g17: یکی here functions as 'a certain' (introducing an indefinite item). نامه بود is a simple past copular clause ('there was a book/letter'), with بود as the past tense of 'to be'. از گه باستان is a prepositional/adverbial phrase 'from the time of antiquity' (گه = 'time/age' in poetic register) modifying the preceding noun phrase.
segments.s382-g18: سخنهای آن = 'its words' (سخنهای plural possessed by the demonstrative/pronoun آن referring back to the book). برمنش راستان is an adjectival description attached to those words: برمنش 'of noble/good disposition' and راستان 'upright/righteous' — together qualifying the content of the book. Word order is noun + possessive + postpositive adjectival phrase, typical in elevated/poetic Persian.
segments.s382-g19: چو introduces a simile ('like/as'). جامی گهر بود is a nominal predicate built as 'a cup of jewel' or 'a jeweled cup' with بود in the past. و منثور بود is a coordinate clause ('and it was prose'), where منثور functions as a predicative adjective/nominal ('prose') and بود again gives the past-state.
segments.s382-g20: طبایع is the subject ('the natures/temperaments'). ز پیوند او is an ablative/source phrase ('from/with its bond/connection') with ز (poetic variant of از). دور بود is a predicate ('was distant/removed'). Overall: 'its dispositions were removed from that bond.'
segments.s382-g21: گذشته is a past participial/adjectival form meaning 'gone/past'. برو here functions adverbially ('over/by') in the phrase گذشته برو = 'having passed by'. سالیان شش هزار places the time-frame after the noun (years + numeral), a common poetic ordering: 'years six thousand' = 'six thousand years.'
segments.s382-g22: گر = 'if'. ایدونک is an archaic/poetic particle meaning roughly 'now/at this point' (here marking the hypothetical timing). پرسش نماید is a causative/performative construction meaning 'would pose/ask a question' (نماید is a third-person subjunctive/optative-ish form used in narrative). شمار is the object 'the count/number' (i.e. 'ask about the number'). The clause is conditional and anticipatory.
segments.s382-g23: This line uses an inverted/elliptical word order: کس گمان نبردی به پیوند او = 'no one took/harbored the thought concerning his link/bond.' نبردی is a past-form verb here (negative sense 'did not take/harbor' in idiom with گمان), به پیوند او is the prepositional phrase 'about/regarding his bond', and کس گمان places 'no one' + 'the thought' as the semantic core. The clause asserts absence of any such suspicion.
segments.s382-g24: پر اندیشه گشت = 'became full of thought' (پر = 'full (of)', اندیشه = 'thought', گشت = 'became'). This is followed by the topicalized subject این دل شادمان = 'this joyful heart' (literally 'this heart, happy'), so the verb phrase precedes the subject for poetic effect: 'this joyful heart became filled with thought.'
segments.s382-g25: گرفتم is the 1st‑person past 'I took/gave' (here 'I offered'); به + noun marks the beneficiary/direction 'to/for the speaker'; بر آفرین is an idiomatic phrase meaning 'an acclamation/praise (said) to' — بر ('toward') links آفرین ('acclamation') to گوینده as what was given.
segments.s382-g26: که introduces the subordinate clause ('for/that'); پیوند را uses را as the definite object marker ('the union'); راه داد is a light‑verb construction 'gave a way' (راه = path/road, داد = gave) and اندرین 'in this/thereby' locates the action in the present context.
segments.s382-g27: اگرچه is the concessive 'although'; نپیوست is the negative past of پیوستن 'he did not join / he joined not' (here 'took part only a little'); جز اندکی 'except a little' restricts the extent of his participation.
segments.s382-g28: ز ... و ز ... is the poetic prepositional repetition 'from/of ... and of ...' (ز رزم و ز بزم = 'in war and in feast'); از هزاران یکی is a standard expression 'one of thousands' (از + plural + یکی), meaning 'one in a thousand'.
segments.s382-g29: همو 'that very one / he himself' is the emphatic subject; بود is the past copula 'was'; گوینده را (را = object/beneficiary marker) places 'for the speaker'; راه بر functions as a nominal predicate 'a way/guidance for' — the sequence subject + بود + indirect object + predicate is standard.
segments.s382-g30: که introduces a relative/cause clause 'who/that'; بنشاند is an archaic/formal verbal form from نشاندن 'to seat/establish' (here 'set up' or 'install'); شاهی is the object 'kingship'; the compound/adjacent phrase ابر گاه‌بر is poetic imagery ('cloud‑...' modifies شاهی), so the clause says he established a lofty/cloud‑like kingship.
segments.s382-g31: همی is a frequentative/adverb meaning 'still/continually'; یافت is past 'obtained' (here transitive 'received'); از مهتران marks the source 'from the elders/superiors'; ارج و گنج are the coordinated direct objects 'honor and treasure.'
segments.s382-g32: ز introduces cause/reason 'because of'; خوی بد خویش is a possessive phrase 'from his bad disposition' with ش as the enclitic possessive 'his'; بودی is the past copula 'he was' and به رنج 'in suffering' is a prepositional phrase expressing resultant state.
segments.s382-g1: چو = 'when' (temporal conjunction) introduces the time clause. این نامه افتاد در دست من is a straightforward past-tense clause (افتاد 'fell') with the locative phrase در دست من 'into my hand' after the verb — typical classical Persian word order allows the verb before or after the locative phrase.
segments.s382-g2: This clause is predicative: شستِ من 'my hand/thumb' is the subject and شد 'became' is the past-tense copula. به ماهی گراینده is a nominal/participial phrase 'one that draws or seeks fish' (literally 'fish-drawing'), used metaphorically; word order places the predicate phrase before the subject for poetic effect.
segments.s382-g3: نگه کردم is a past-tense verbal phrase 'I looked/examined' (نگه 'look' + کردم 'I did'). این نظم سست آمدم uses آمدم in a first-person past sense meaning 'it came to me' or idiomatically 'it seemed to me'; نظم here means 'verse/poem' and سست is an adjectival predicate 'weak'.
segments.s382-g4: بسی بیت is a poetic quantifier + noun: بسی 'many/very' (here used as 'many') and بیت 'couplets'. ناتندرست آمدم repeats the same idiomatic use of آمدم 'it seemed to me / I found' with ناتندرست 'unsound/defective' as the predicate describing those couplets.
segments.s382-g5: من این زان بگفتم is a past declarative: 'I said this' (بگفتم). زان here means 'for that reason / from that' (linking to what was just observed). که تا شهریار begins the subordinate purpose/result clause: که introduces a content clause and تا signals purpose 'so that' with شهریار as the subject of that clause.
segments.s382-g6: بداند is a third-person present (subjunctive/optative) meaning 'may know' or 'should know' used after تا to express purpose. The clause is verbless in the modern sense: سخن گفتن نابکار has the infinitival/masdar-like گفتن 'to speak' with سخن 'speech' and نابکار 'wrong/evil' as the predicate — literally 'that speaking (i.e. such speech) is wicked/incorrect.'
segments.s382-g7: This phrase is elliptical and poetic. دو گوهر بد این literally 'these two jewels are bad' (دو گوهر = two jewels, بد = bad, این = these). با دو گوهر فروش is a compact phrase 'with/to the jewel-seller' — فروش is used as 'seller' (or the one involved in selling), so the whole line functions like an instruction 'sell these two bad jewels to the jeweller' though the imperative sense is left implied by the poetic diction.
segments.s382-g8: کنون شاه دارد is present-tense 'now the king has/holds' with دارد used idiomatically. به گفتار گوش is an idiom meaning 'an ear to speech' i.e. 'listens to speech' or 'pays heed to such talk' (به + noun + گوش = 'gives ear to ...'). Word order places the object-complement phrase after دارد.
segments.s382-g57: This is a coordinated list of four abstract qualities introduced by repeated به, which here functions as an adverbial/prepositional marker meaning roughly ‘in’ or ‘by (the quality of)’. Each noun stands as an abstract quality (بخش ‘generosity/gift’, داد ‘justice’, رای ‘counsel/plan’, هنر ‘virtue/excellence’) and the conjunction و simply links them. The repeated به gives a balanced, emphatic poetic rhythm.
segments.s382-g58: نبد is an archaic/polite past-negative form (≈ نبُود/نبود) meaning ‘there was not’ or ‘there was none’; it introduces the main assertion. تاج را is the definite object marked by را. ز + enclitic (زو) is the poetic ablative ‘from/of him’ (i.e. ‘than him’ in a comparative sense), and سزاوارتر is a comparative adjective formed with the -تر suffix meaning ‘more deserving/fit’. The clause says literally ‘There was not the crown more deserving from him’ → ‘no one was more deserving of the crown than he.’
segments.s382-g59: بیامد (simple past ‘he came’) followed immediately by نشست (simple past ‘he sat’) is a common narrative sequence—two consecutive actions. از بر تخت is a slightly archaic/prepositional phrase meaning ‘upon/on the throne’ (بر = ‘on’, از here used poetically with location), and داد modifies تخت as ‘throne of Justice’ (noun+noun genitive without an ezāfe in older style).
segments.s382-g60: جهاندار is a title/noun ‘sovereign’ or ‘world-ruler’. چون او ‘like him’ is a comparative phrase (چون = ‘like’). ندارد is the present-tense verb ‘has not’ (here idiomatically meaning ‘there is not’ or ‘no one equals’), and به یاد (‘in memory’) is a prepositional phrase meaning ‘remembered’ or ‘kept in memory’. Together: ‘A sovereign like him is not remembered’ / ‘There is no sovereign like him in memory.’
segments.s382-g61: ز شاهان is an ablative/relational phrase ‘from/of the kings’ (i.e. ‘among/compared with kings’). پیشی is an abstract noun ‘preeminence/precedence’. همی بگذرد is an older continuous/present form: همی (an older pre-verbal particle similar to modern می) + بگذرد (3rd person present/subjunctive stem of گذشتن) meaning ‘he passes/exceeds’ or ‘he surpasses’. The clause says he ‘surpasses the kings in precedence’.
segments.s382-g62: نفس داستان را is the object phrase: نفس here is used metaphorically as ‘breath’ (a small unit of life or moment) and داستان ‘the tale/epic’; را marks the definite object. همی (progressive particle) + نشمرد is the negative/negated form of شمردن ‘to count/reckon’ (ن- can be part of the negative or of the verb form in classical usage), giving the sense ‘does not count/does not reckon’. So the clause means literally ‘the tale does not count the breath (of him)’ — i.e. the story cannot be measured by single breaths / the tale does not reckon his (many) deeds by mere breaths.
segments.s382-g63: چه ... چه here forms a parallel rhetorical pair (‘what ... what ...’) contrasting two things (دینار ‘coin/money’ and خاک ‘dust/earth’). بر چشم او is an idiom meaning ‘in his sight/regard’ with بر = ‘on/upon’ used for ‘to the eye’. The sequence contrasts riches and insignificance to emphasize indifference: ‘What coin in his sight, what dust (does it matter)?’
segments.s382-g64: به رزم و به بزم repeats the prepositional به to list two contexts: ‘in battle and in banquet’ (رزم = battle, بزم = feast/assembly). اندرش is the compound of اندر ‘in/within’ + the clitic ش (= his), giving ‘in him’. نیست باک is an existential negation meaning ‘there is no fear’ or ‘he has no concern’. Put together: ‘In battle and at feasts there is no fear in him’ — i.e. he fears nothing in either situation.
segments.s382-g41: مرا is the experiencer (to/for me) fronted for emphasis; این سخن is the topical subject that follows it. بر دل is a prepositional phrase ('upon the heart') marking the sphere affected, and آسان نبود is the predication with نبود as the past negative copula ('was not easy'). Word order is emphatic/inverted compared with modern colloquial Persian (experiencer + topic + locative + predicate).
segments.s382-g42: بجز is a preposition 'except' introducing the sole exception; خامشی ('silence') is the noun that follows. هیچ درمان نبود is an existential negative: هیچ ('no/none') negates درمان ('remedy'), and نبود ('was not') is the past existential/catenative verb ('there was no cure'). The whole clause states that silence was the only possible remedy.
segments.s382-g43: نشستنگه is a compound noun meaning a sitting-place / gathering-place; it heads the noun phrase. مردم نیک‌بخت follows as the group being described ('the fortunate people') with the adjective نیک‌بخت postposed, a common Persian order (noun + adjective) in older/poetic usage.
segments.s382-g44: یکی is an indefinite determiner 'a certain/one' placed before باغ ('garden'). دیدم is the past first-person verb 'I saw' (SOV order: object(s) + verb). سراسر درخت is a compressed adjectival phrase 'throughout (it) trees' or 'entirely of trees' modifying باغ; the linking ezafe is omitted for poetic compactness.
segments.s382-g45: به جایی here conveys 'in/at any place' or 'nowhere' depending on the negative that follows. نبد is the archaic past-negative copula 'was not / did not exist' (compare نبود). هیچ پیدا ('nothing visible') is the negated noun phrase, and درش is the locative 'in it' with the clitic -ش ('its/in it'). The clause thus states an absence of anything visible inside/anywhere in it.
segments.s382-g46: بجز introduces an exception: 'except'. نام شاهی can be read as 'the name/reputation of kingship' (نام = name/reputation; شاهی = kingship). نبد is again the past negative 'was not', and افسرش ('its afsar') means 'its helmet/diadem' with the pronominal suffix -ش. The clause says that except for the royal name, its diadem/crown was not present.
segments.s382-g47: که‌گر (archaic compound of که + گر) introduces a conditional 'that if'. در خور is an idiomatic phrase meaning 'worthy/fit for' (here fused as در خور باغ 'fit for the garden'); in the tokenization provided خور does not carry a matching listed sense so it is marked -1. بایستمی is an older/poetic first-person form with the -می ending; here it expresses a conditional/hypothetical 'I would stand / I would be placed' or 'I should stand' (modal sense).
segments.s382-g48: اگر is the ordinary conditional 'if'. نیک بودی uses the past subjunctive/conditional form 'if you were good' (بودی = 'were'). بشایستمی is a conditional/optative first-person form built on شایست (to befit): the prefix ب- and the -می ending produce 'I would befit / I would deserve' (i.e. 'I would be worthy'). The two clauses form a typical protasis–apodosis conditional sequence.
segments.s382-g9: سخن is the topic 'speech' or 'what is said'. چو بدین گونه is an adverbial phrase ('if/when in this way'); بدین here is the compound prepositional/demonstrative sense 'in/with this' and گونه 'manner'. بایدت is the modal 'it is necessary/you must' with the enclitic ـت marking 'you', and گفت functions as the verbal noun/infinitive sense 'to say' (so بایدت گفت = 'you must say').
segments.s382-g10: مگو و مکن are two negative imperatives: 'do not say and do not do'. طبع 'temperament/nature' is the patient/topic of the prohibition, با introduces accompaniment ('with'), and رنج جفت literally 'pair with hardship' — جفت is used in the sense 'pair/join' (here urging not to join your nature with suffering).
segments.s382-g11: چو = 'when/if'. بند روان is a genitival phrase 'the bond/fetter of the soul' (بند = bond, روان = soul), بینی is second-person singular present 'you see'. و رنج تن parallels the previous phrase: 'and the suffering of the body' (رنج = suffering, تن = body).
segments.s382-g12: به کانی = 'to/unto a mine' (به + کانی). که introduces a relative clause 'which/where' and گوهر نیابی = 'you will not find a jewel' (نیابی here is the 2nd-person negative form of یافتن/یابیدن), while the final مکن is the negative imperative 'do not (do that)' — i.e. 'do not go to such a mine'.
segments.s382-g13: چو ... نباشد = 'if there be not' (conditional). طبعی = 'a temperament/natural bent'. چو آب روان is a simile 'like/as flowing water' (چو = 'as/like', آب روان = 'running/flowing water'), so the whole phrase says 'if there is no disposition like flowing water'.
segments.s382-g14: مبر is the negative imperative 'do not go/take'. سوی = 'toward', این نامه خسروان = 'this book/epistle of kings' (نامه used here in the sense of a book or treatise concerning kings). The clause warns not to approach/offer oneself to that book (i.e. to the court/subject).
segments.s382-g15: دهن‌گر here refers to the speaker/one who uses the mouth; بماند is 'remains' or 'is left'. ز خوردن تهی is a prepositional phrase 'empty from eating' = 'hungry' or 'without having eaten'. The segment sets the condition 'if the speaker's mouth remains empty (i.e. if one is unfed)'.
segments.s382-g16: ازان به که is an idiomatic classical construction meaning 'from that it is better that' (i.e. 'therefore it is better that'). ناساز خوانی نهی: ناساز 'out of tune / discordant', خوانی is 'singing/recital', and نهی is a prohibition (or injunction) — so the clause says one should forbid/is better to forbid discordant singing (rather than perform it in hunger).
segments.s382-g33: ستاینده is an agent noun meaning “one who praises / a flatterer.” It governs the following noun phrase شهریاران بدی, where شهریاران is the plural noun “kings” and بدی is a postpositive qualifier meaning “badness/vice.” Together شهریاران بدی = “wicked/bad kings,” so the whole segment reads “a praiser (flatterer) of wicked kings.”
segments.s382-g34: به introduces a locative/directional phrase (“to/into/at”), here combining with کاخ “palace.” افسر (“helmet, crest”) is used metaphorically as an ornament or emblem of the palace. نامداران بدی is a genitive/qualifying group: نامداران “the renowned” with بدی qualifying them (“in their evil” / “renowned evildoers”). The phrase thus pictures a palace-helm (an emblem) associated with the renowned in their evil.
segments.s382-g35: به شهر اندرون is a directional/locative phrase “into the city’s inner quarters.” گشته is a perfect participle (“having gone / entered”), and گشتی is a verbal/adjectival form meaning “roving/going about.” In Persian poetic order the verbal cluster comes before the noun it concerns, so سخن (the subject “word/rumor”) is placed after them: literally, “into the inner city having gone and roaming — the word,” i.e. “the word/rumor went into the inner city and spread.”
segments.s382-g36: ازو means “from him / because of him.” نو شدی is a verbal phrase where نو “new” combines with شدی (past of شد, “became”) to give “became new.” روزگار کهن is the noun phrase “the ancient times/age,” with کهن postpositively qualifying روزگار. Together: “From him the old times became new.”
segments.s382-g37: من این نامه فرخ گرفتم به فال is a straightforward declarative clause. من “I” is subject, این نامه “this letter” is the object, فرخ (“fortunate, auspicious”) qualifies نامه, and گرفتم is the 1st‑person past “I took.” به فال is an idiomatic complement meaning “as an omen” (lit. “to/for omen”).
segments.s382-g38: بسی رنج بردم is the core: بسی (an intensive adverb) + رنج “suffering” + بردم (1st‑person past of بردن, here “I bore/underwent”). به بسیار سال gives the duration “for many years” (به marking the timeframe). So: “I endured great suffering for many years.”
segments.s382-g39: ندیدم is the 1st‑person past negative “I did not see.” It is followed by the nominal phrase سرافراز بخشنده‌ای: سرافراز “proud/honored/lofty” qualifies بخشنده‌ای, where بخشنده (with the indefinite suffix ‑ای) means “a giver / generous one.” The structure: “I did not see a lofty, generous man.”
segments.s382-g40: به گاه is a temporal prepositional phrase “at the time / in the days of.” کیان‌بر is a compound title/name meaning “bearer/supporter of the kings (a dynastic title or epithet)” and stands as the temporal reference; درخشنده‌ای (indefinite) means “a shining/radiant one.” The whole gives a parallel idea: “nor, in the time of the king‑bearer, a radiant one.”

sentences.s382-s33: Sometimes gold for the feast and sometimes a sword for battle — he never withholds (them) from the one who asks.
sentences.s382-s25: When I laid aside my speech in the twentieth year, to learn who deserves this hardship.
sentences.s382-s26: Abu'l-Qasim, that sovereign of the world, from whom the imperial crown was newly made.
sentences.s382-s27: The sovereign Mahmud, with glory and generosity, so that even the moon and Kiwan make prostration to him.
sentences.s382-s28: At the head of the book his name became a crown; on the heart's dark carpet it turned like ivory.
sentences.s382-s9: There was a certain book from ancient times; its words were of noble, upright character.
sentences.s382-s10: It was like a jeweled cup and was prose; its natures were far removed from its bond.
sentences.s382-s11: Six thousand years have passed by; if one were to ask now about the count...
sentences.s382-s12: No one entertained the thought of his bond; this joyful heart became full of thought.
sentences.s382-s13: I gave the speaker my praise, for he thereby opened a way for the union.
sentences.s382-s14: Although he joined only a little, in war and in feast he was one of a thousand.
sentences.s382-s15: He himself was the guide for the speaker, who thereby set up a kingship lofty as a cloud.
sentences.s382-s16: He continually obtained honor and treasure from the elders, but because of his bad disposition he suffered hardship.
sentences.s382-s1: When this letter fell into my hand, my hand became like a fish-catcher.
sentences.s382-s2: I examined it; the verse seemed weak, and I found many couplets unsound.
sentences.s382-s3: I said this so that the king might know that such speech is improper.
sentences.s382-s4: Sell these two bad jewels to the jeweller; now the king lends an ear to such talk.
sentences.s382-s29: In generosity, in justice, in counsel and in excellence there was no one more worthy of the crown than he.
sentences.s382-s30: He came and sat upon the throne of Justice; a sovereign like him is not remembered.
sentences.s382-s31: He surpasses the kings in preeminence; the story does not count (even) his breath.
sentences.s382-s32: What dinar in his eyes, what dust — in battle and in banquet there is no fear in him.
sentences.s382-s21: This saying was not easy on my heart; there was no remedy except silence.
sentences.s382-s22: At the seat/gathering of the fortunate people I saw a certain garden, entirely full of trees.
sentences.s382-s23: There was nowhere anything visible in it; except for the name of kingship, its diadem (helmet) did not exist.
sentences.s382-s24: For if I were to stand in the fitting place of the garden — if you were good, I would be worthy of it.
sentences.s382-s5: If speech must be put in this manner, do not say (it) and do not pair your temperament with hardship.
sentences.s382-s6: When you see the soul's fetter and the body's suffering, do not go to a mine where you will not find a jewel.
sentences.s382-s7: If there is no temperament like flowing water, do not go toward this book of kings.
sentences.s382-s8: If the mouth remains empty from eating, it is better, for that reason, that you forbid discordant singing.
sentences.s382-s17: A flatterer of wicked kings — a palace helm for the renowned in their evil.
sentences.s382-s18: The rumor entered the city's inner quarters and went roaming; from him the ancient era was made new.
sentences.s382-s19: I took this fortunate letter as an omen; I endured much suffering for many years.
sentences.s382-s20: I saw no proud, generous man, nor (in the days of the king‑bearer) a radiant one.
